title: Air-fryer courgette fries
subtitle: Panko-crusted courgette batons, crisp from the air fryer.
excerpt: Courgette cut into thick batons, coated in seasoned panko breadcrumbs with parmesan and baked in the air fryer until golden and crunchy. A much better use of summer courgettes than another ratatouille. Serves two to four as a side.
sourceNotes: Air-fryer courgette fries. Breaded and fried vegetables are documented across Italian and Mediterranean cooking, where zucchini fritti appear as a traditional antipasto. The air-fryer method achieves a similar crust with less oil. The combination of panko breadcrumbs and parmesan creates a crust with more flavour than plain breadcrumbs alone.

content.text: Salt the courgette batons and leave them for 10 minutes before coating; this draws out moisture that would otherwise steam the crumb off. Pat completely dry after salting. Cook in a single layer; courgettes release water as they cook and will steam if crowded.

items.prepNote: cut into batons, about 1 cm wide

content.text: Toss {{courgette}} of courgette batons with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and leave for 10 minutes. Pat thoroughly dry with paper towels. Mix {{panko}} of panko with {{parmesan}} of parmesan and {{garlic-powder}} of garlic powder. Dip each baton in {{plain-flour}} of flour, then {{eggs}} beaten egg, then press firmly into the panko mixture. Arrange in a single layer in the air fryer and spray with cooking oil. Air-fry at 200°C for 10 to 12 minutes until golden, turning once halfway.

items.cause: Courgettes not dried, or overcrowded air fryer.
items.fix: Pat the courgettes completely dry after salting. Cook in a single layer; batch if needed.

items.cause: Flour step skipped, or courgettes too wet.
items.fix: The flour is the key adhesion layer. Dry thoroughly and coat in order: flour, egg, crumbs.
Cut batons at least 1 cm thick. Check at 10 minutes; they should have colour but still hold their shape.

content.text: Air-fryer courgette fries are a summer side dish or snack, made when courgettes are cheap and plentiful. They work as a side to grilled fish or chicken, or on their own with a yoghurt dip. The parmesan in the crumb is the difference between a dull coating and a savoury, toasty one.
